learning_objectives: Define international studies as an interdisciplinary field
Identify the major subfields: political science, economics, history, and culture
Understand why global literacy matters in the 21st century
readings: Smallman & Brown, Introduction to International and Global Studies Ch. 1
UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights (Preamble)

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Introduction to International Studies. This course provides a broad survey of the forces shaping the modern world, from geopolitics to globalization.
key_concepts: Interdisciplinary nature of international studies
State sovereignty and the Westphalian system
Globalization as a defining theme
Actors in the international arena: states, IGOs, NGOs, MNCs
Levels of analysis: individual, state, systemic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive mapping exercise: identifying current global hotspots and the disciplines needed to understand them
setup_requirements: World map or digital atlas access

review.value: The principle established in 1648 that each state has exclusive authority over its territory and domestic affairs
Individual (leaders), state (domestic politics), and systemic (international structure) perspectives for explaining world events

class_schedule.topic: Historical Foundations of the Modern International System
learning_objectives: Trace the evolution from empires to nation-states
Explain the significance of the Treaty of Westphalia
Identify major turning points: colonialism, World Wars, decolonization

lecture_notes.introduction: The international system we inhabit today was not inevitable. Understanding its historical roots is essential for grasping current dynamics.
key_concepts: Pre-Westphalian orders: empires and city-states
Treaty of Westphalia (1648) and sovereign equality
Concert of Europe and balance of power
Colonialism and its lasting legacies
The World Wars and the birth of international institutions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline exercise: mapping key events from 1648 to the founding of the United Nations in 1945

review.value: A system where states align to prevent any single state from dominating, exemplified by the Concert of Europe after 1815
The post-WWII process through which colonial territories gained independence, fundamentally reshaping the international system

learning_objectives: Define economic, political, cultural, and technological globalization
Evaluate arguments for and against globalization
Analyze globalization's uneven impacts across regions
readings: Steger, Globalization: A Very Short Introduction Ch. 1-3
Friedman, The World Is Flat Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Globalization is perhaps the most discussed phenomenon in international studies. We will examine its multiple dimensions and contested meanings.
key_concepts: Economic globalization: trade, finance, supply chains
Political globalization: international governance and norms
Cultural globalization: media, migration, identity
Technological globalization: internet, communication, AI
The anti-globalization critique: inequality and sovereignty loss
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: tracking the supply chain of a smartphone from raw materials to consumer

review.value: The integration of national economies through trade, investment, and capital flows, accelerated by reduced trade barriers and digital technology
The spread of ideas, values, and cultural products across borders, raising debates about homogenization versus hybridization

learning_objectives: Distinguish between IGOs, NGOs, and informal governance mechanisms
Evaluate the effectiveness of the United Nations system
Analyze the role of international law in ordering state behavior

lecture_notes.introduction: States do not act alone. A dense web of institutions shapes international behavior, from the UN Security Council to grassroots NGOs.
key_concepts: Types of international organizations: IGOs and NGOs
The United Nations system: General Assembly, Security Council, specialized agencies
Regional organizations: EU, AU, ASEAN
International law: treaties, customary law, jus cogens
Challenges of enforcement in an anarchic system
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulation: a simplified UN Security Council debate on a current crisis
setup_requirements: Role cards for Security Council members

review.value: Organizations created by treaty among sovereign states (e.g., UN, WTO, NATO) with varying degrees of authority
Peremptory norms of international law from which no derogation is permitted, such as prohibitions on genocide and slavery

learning_objectives: Measure and compare global inequality using GDP, HDI, and Gini coefficients
Contrast modernization theory with dependency theory
Evaluate the Sustainable Development Goals as a framework
readings: Sachs, The End of Poverty Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Why are some countries rich and others poor? This question drives much of international studies, and the answers are deeply contested.
key_concepts: Measuring development: GDP per capita, HDI, Gini coefficient
Modernization theory: development as stages of growth
Dependency theory: structural exploitation in the global economy
World-systems theory: core, periphery, semi-periphery
Sustainable Development Goals: a universal agenda
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data analysis exercise: comparing development indicators across 10 countries using UNDP data

review.value: A composite index measuring life expectancy, education, and per capita income, offering a broader view than GDP alone
The argument that underdevelopment is not a stage but a structural condition created by exploitative relationships between core and peripheral countries

class_schedule.topic: Contemporary Global Challenges and the Future
learning_objectives: Identify emerging global challenges: climate change, pandemics, cyber threats, AI governance
Assess how existing institutions cope with transnational problems
Articulate a personal framework for global citizenship
readings: IPCC Sixth Assessment Report Summary for Policymakers (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The 21st century presents challenges that no single state can solve alone. We close this introductory survey by looking ahead.
key_concepts: Climate change as a collective action problem
Global health governance: lessons from COVID-19
Cybersecurity and digital sovereignty
Artificial intelligence and international norms
The concept of global citizenship
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured debate: which global challenge deserves the most international attention and resources?
setup_requirements: Current data on selected global challenges

review.value: A situation where all parties would benefit from cooperation but individual incentives discourage it, exemplified by climate change mitigation
The idea that individuals have responsibilities beyond their national borders, grounded in shared humanity and interconnectedness

quizzes.topic: Foundations of International Studies and the International System

questions.question: Explain the significance of the Treaty of Westphalia (1648) for the modern international system. How does the concept of sovereignty it established still shape international relations today?
questions.answer: The Treaty of Westphalia ended the Thirty Years' War and established the principle of state sovereignty, meaning each state has supreme authority within its territory and is legally equal to other states. This remains foundational today: sovereignty underpins the UN Charter, non-intervention norms, and the legal equality of states, though globalization and human rights norms increasingly challenge absolute sovereignty.

questions.question: Define the three levels of analysis used in international studies (individual, state, systemic) and provide one example of how each level would explain the outbreak of a war.
questions.answer: Individual level: a leader's personality or misperception (e.g., Saddam Hussein's miscalculation in invading Kuwait). State level: domestic politics, regime type, or nationalism driving aggression (e.g., diversionary war theory). Systemic level: the structure of the international system, such as a power vacuum or alliance commitments triggering conflict (e.g., the alliance system in WWI).

rubric.key: Provides relevant example for each level
Examples demonstrate clear understanding of distinctions

questions.question: Compare and contrast economic globalization and cultural globalization. How might they reinforce or conflict with each other?
questions.answer: Economic globalization involves the integration of markets through trade, investment, and supply chains, while cultural globalization involves the spread of ideas, media, and cultural practices across borders. They reinforce each other when trade spreads cultural products (e.g., Hollywood films, fast food), but they can conflict when economic integration threatens local cultures, provoking backlash against foreign cultural influence (e.g., resistance to Western media in parts of Asia and the Middle East).

rubric.key: Explains how they reinforce each other

questions.question: What is the difference between an intergovernmental organization (IGO) and a non-governmental organization (NGO)? Give one example of each and explain its role in global governance.
questions.answer: An IGO is created by treaty among sovereign states and operates through state representatives (e.g., the United Nations coordinates international peace and security, development, and humanitarian action). An NGO is a private, non-profit organization that operates across borders without government mandate (e.g., Amnesty International monitors human rights abuses, publishes reports, and lobbies governments). IGOs have formal authority derived from states; NGOs influence through advocacy, expertise, and public pressure.

rubric.key: Explains their respective roles in governance

major_assignments.title: Global Issue Analysis Paper
major_assignments.description: Select a contemporary global issue and analyze it through at least three disciplinary lenses (political, economic, cultural, historical, environmental). Produce a 2000-word analytical paper that demonstrates interdisciplinary thinking.
objectives: Apply multiple disciplinary perspectives to a single issue
Synthesize information from academic and policy sources
Practice proper academic writing and citation
deliverables: Annotated bibliography with at least 8 scholarly sources

criteria: Issue is clearly defined and scoped
At least three disciplinary perspectives are applied
Clear thesis statement and logical structure
Free of grammatical and spelling errors
Sophisticated interdisciplinary analysis of a well-defined global issue
Strong evidence base from diverse scholarly sources
Clear, professional writing with proper citations

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular quizzes assessing comprehension of readings and lecture material

participation.description: Active engagement in class discussions, simulations, and group activities

major_assignments.description: Global issue analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering foundational concepts and theories

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam emphasizing application of course concepts to real-world scenarios

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a 5% grade deduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers lose 5% per day. Extensions must be requested at least 48 hours in advance with valid justification.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussions and study groups are encouraged. All written work must be individually authored. Proper citation is required for all sources.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism or fabrication of sources will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board. AI tools may be used for research assistance but not for generating submitted text.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to a computer with internet for research, university library databases, and the course learning management system.
